sevenDayForecast.headline: Ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle is expected to re-intensify into a tropical cyclone during Tuesday night.
points: Tropical Cyclone Advices and Forecast Track Maps are being issued and should be referred to for more detailed information.
Ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle is moving off the west Kimberley coast this morning. There is a High chance of it redeveloping into a tropical cyclone over the Indian Ocean during Tuesday night.

The system is forecast to move west southwest, roughly parallel to the Pilbara coast on Wednesday and into Thursday as a severe tropical cyclone. From late Thursday or early Friday, Narelle is expected to start turning towards the southeast and is likely to impact western parts of WA by late in the week.

trackForecast.headline: Ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle (34U) is moving offshore of the west Kimberley coast and is expected to redevelop into a tropical cyclone during Tuesday night.
trackForecast.discussion:
Ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle is moving offshore of the west Kimberley coast and towards the west southwest. The environment is favourable and it will likely re-intensify into a tropical cyclone during Tuesday night. Accordingly, there is a risk of gales affecting the west Kimberley coast before the system moves further offshore.

Model guidance is generally consistent in developing Narelle into a severe tropical cyclone by Wednesday afternoon. It should continue to move towards the west-southwest during Wednesday and Thursday, roughly parallel to the Pilbara coast. The extent of gales to the south of Narelle is likely to be larger than normal, and the most likely scenario has gales extending to coastal parts of the Pilbara, even while the system remains well offshore.

In the longer term, Narelle is expected to take a gradual turn towards the southeast. Depending on the timing of this turn, it may move close to or over the west Pilbara coast on Friday, or may pass west of Exmouth. After turning to the south and then southeast the environment starts to become less favourable, and along with possible land interactions weakening is expected to commence. Significant uncertainty remains at this lead time.
trackForecast.discussionUpperBound: Upper bound figures off the Pilbara coast reflect a more rapid intensification, reaching category 5 intensity.
